chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-1. Purpose
chunks.text:
This regulation prescribes the officer transfers from active duty (AD) to the Reserve Component (RC) and discharge
functions for all officers on AD for 30 days or more. It provides principles of support, standards of service, and policies
to support office transfers and discharges.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-2. References and forms
chunks.text: See appendix A.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-3. Explanation of abbreviations and terms
chunks.text: See the glossary.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-4. Responsibilities
chunks.text:
Assistant Secretary of the Army (Manpower and Reserve Affairs) (ASA (M&RA)) will develop and oversee
policies related to officer retention, transition, and separation. The ASA (M&RA)’s responsibilities will be accom-
plished through the Deputy Assistant Secretary of the Army (Review Boards) (DASA (RB)), who will act as the
Secretary of the Army (SECARMY)’s designee in involuntary release from active duty (REFRAD) of officers under
the provisions of chapter 2 of this regulation (through the Director, Military Review Boards), and for officer elimina-
tions under the provisions of chapter 4 of this regulation, resignations and discharges in lieu of elimination, retirements
in lieu of elimination, resignation for the good of the Service (RFGOS) in lieu of court-martial, and will make final
determinations in each case in accordance with a delegation of authority by SECARMY. This regulation does not
constitute a separate delegation of authority by SECARMY to the DASA (RB), but rather provides guidance in the
administrative execution of that delegated authority by the Army Review Boards Agency (ARBA).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-4. Responsibilities
chunks.text:
Deputy Chief of Staff (DCS), G - 1 will establish personnel policies relating to officer transfers and discharges
and through the Commanding General (CG), Human Resources Command (HRC), will establish standards and oper-
ating tasks for the officer transfer and discharge program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-4. Responsibilities
chunks.text:
The Judge Advocate General (TJAG) will, upon request, review Department of the Army (DA) administrative
changes to regulations to verify the legality of prescribed policies and changes, and through the servicing Office of
Staff Judge Advocate, will ensure sufficient trained, qualified, and certified legal personnel are available to support
Boards of Inquiry, as needed, and will provide the recorder, assistant recorder (if any), and legal advisor to Boards of
Inquiry.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-4. Responsibilities
chunks.text:
CG, U.S. Army Training and Doctrine Command (TRADOC), through the Commandant, Adjutant General
School, will ensure lesson programs of instruction incorporate the provisions of this regulation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-4. Responsibilities
chunks.text:
The Chief, National Guard Bureau (NGB) will execute policies for Army National Guard of the United States
(ARNGUS) officer transfers, discharges, and retirements under the provisions of this regulation. The Chief, NGB may
delegate, if necessary, in writing, the authority to act on his behalf to a lower authority within the NGB.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-4. Responsibilities
chunks.text:
Commanders of Army commands (ACOMs), Army service component commands (ASCCs), and direct reporting
units (DRUs) will monitor the administration of the officer transfer and discharge program to ensure compliance with
the policies and operating tasks established by this regulation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-5. Records management (recordkeeping) requirements
chunks.text:
The records management requirement for all record numbers, associated forms, and reports required by this regulation
are addressed in the Army Records Retention Schedule-Army (RRS - A). Detailed information for all related record
numbers, forms, and reports are located in ARIMS/RRS - A at https://www.arims.army.mil. If any record numbers,
forms, and reports are not current, addressed, and/or published correctly in ARIMS/RRS - A, see DA Pam 25 - 403 for
guidance.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-6. Statutory authority
chunks.text:
Officer transfers and discharges are closely governed by various statutes and by DOD directives and instructions.
Personnel involved in officer transfers and discharges should remain alert to changes in the governing statutes and
DOD directives and instructions as those authorities take precedence over this regulation. Where governing statutes
or DOD directives and instructions supersede specific provisions of this regulation, the unaffected provisions of this
regulation will remain in force.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-7. Manpower requirements
chunks.text:
Manpower requirements for the officer transfer and discharge functions will be determined by the appropriate require-
ments procedures process. Requirements for table of distribution and allowances (TDA) organizations are determined
by the Manpower Staffing Standard System and Manpower Surveys. Requirements for table of organization and
equipment (TOE) and modification table of organization and equipment (MTOE) are determined by manpower re-
quirements criteria. The peacetime mission availability factor provides the link between TDA and TOE/MTOE or-
ganizations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text:
Most personnel work in the field is performed at three primary levels: unit, battalion (BN), and installation (or some
equivalent of an installation).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text: Unit- and BN-level work is performed at these respective levels.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text:
Installation-level work requires an understanding of the organizational structure. Work at the installation falls
into two major categories:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text:
Command and Staff (C&S) work includes that work required to manage the personnel strength of a command
and to support the commander’s decision process. The headquarters elements of all ACOMs are responsible for C&S
work (brigades within Army divisions are notable exceptions). C&S work includes the release from AD, discharge,
retirements, eliminations, special correspondence, and personnel actions requiring a commander’s decision.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text:
Personnel support work is that work performed by either the Military Personnel Division (MPD) or as a part of
the base operations support structure. For U.S. Army Reserve (USAR) active guard reserve (AGR) Soldiers, personnel
support work is performed by personnel service support teams (PSSTs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text: The guidance in all chapters will address the following levels of work:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text: Soldier. Work beginning with input from a Soldier.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text: Unit. Work executed at unit level.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text: Battalion. Work executed at BN level.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text:
Command and Staff. Work executed within the chain of command and by staff elements (other than the BN or
brigade within a division).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-8. Levels of work
chunks.text:
Personnel support. Work executed in a personnel support organization. The specific personnel support work
centers required to perform the work for manpower purposes covered by this regulation are Personnel Plans and Ac-
tions (PPAAs), transition center (TC), and Soldier actions (SACTs).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Officer transfer/discharge process
chunks.text: The officer transfer/discharge process is divided into the following six subfunctions:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Officer transfer/discharge process
chunks.text: Voluntary REFRAD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Officer transfer/discharge process
chunks.text: Involuntary REFRAD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Officer transfer/discharge process
chunks.text: Resignations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Officer transfer/discharge process
chunks.text: Eliminations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Officer transfer/discharge process
chunks.text: Miscellaneous types of separation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-9. Officer transfer/discharge process
chunks.text: Retirements.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
All officer separation requests and elimination actions (except retirement applications unless noted) will be forwarded
to the following appropriate addresses or as required elsewhere in this regulation:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
General officers, Army competitive category colonels, and promotable lieutenant colonels: Office of the Chief
of Staff (DACS - GOM), 200 Army Pentagon, Washington, DC 20310 - 0200 (also retirement applications).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
Chaplains: Office of the Chief of Chaplains (DACH - PER), 2700 Army Pentagon, Washington, DC
20310 - 2700.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
Officers of The Judge Advocate General’s Corps (JAGC): The Judge Advocate General (DAJA - PT), 2200
Army Pentagon, Washington, DC 20310 - 2200.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
Officers not assigned to a branch of service in paragraphs 1-10a through 1-10c: Commander, Human Resources
Command (AHRC - OPD - A), 1600 Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
Retirement applications (except as noted in paras 1-10a and 1-10f) will be forwarded through the addresses
listed in paragraphs 1-10b through 1-10d, as applicable, to the Commander, Human Resources Command
(AHRC - OPD - A), 1600 Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
All USAR AGR officer separation and retirement applications: to the Commander, Human Resources Command
(AHRC - AR), 1600 Spearhead Division Road, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400. Elimination actions will be processed by
the CG, HRC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
All Army National Guard (ARNG) Title 10, United States Code (USC) AGR officer separations and retirement
applications: Army National Guard (ARNG - HCM), 111 S. George Mason Drive, Arlington, VA 22204 - 1373.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-10. Headquarters, Department of the Army addresses for officers
chunks.text:
All ARNG 32 USC AGR officer separations and retirement applications: the appropriate state adjutant general’s
office.
Section II
Principles and Standards

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Principles of support
chunks.text: The military personnel system will direct a function to-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Principles of support
chunks.text:
Provide a mechanism to terminate the services of an officer prior to the terms of the original contract (both
voluntarily and involuntarily).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Principles of support
chunks.text: Provide authority to transfer officers from one component to another.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Principles of support
chunks.text: Provide authority to discharge officers from all military obligations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-11. Principles of support
chunks.text: Support the Army’s personnel life-cycle function of transition.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text: The officer transfer/discharge program is-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text: A wartime military personnel function.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text: Provided its function resources by the TOE.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text:
The functional responsibility of the Personnel Operations Branch and PPAA work center (and their tactical
counterpart).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text:
Involuntary transfers and discharges will be processed to completion without delay upon initiation by the com-
mander.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text: Wartime standards will be extended or curtailed as follows:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text:
Terms of service will be extended by the President and/or Congress through declaration of national emergency
or war.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-12. Standards of service
chunks.text:
Transfer and discharge authority (voluntary and involuntary) will be curtailed by SECARMY.
Section III
Scope

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text:
An officer may request separation from the Army under the provisions of this regulation. The appropriate com-
mander will ensure that the officer is separated on the date specified in the separation order and furnish the discharge
certificate specified by CG, HRC (if appropriate).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: A request for separation may be disapproved by the approval authority when the-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: Officer is under investigation or charges.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: Officer is awaiting result of trial.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text:
Officer is being considered for involuntary separation (Department of the Army Active Duty Board (DAADB)
or elimination).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: Officer is absent without leave (AWOL).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: Officer is under control of civil authorities.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: Officer is mentally incompetent.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: Officer is in default with respect to public property or funds.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: Officer has not fulfilled an active duty service obligation (ADSO) specified in AR 350 - 100.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text:
Officer has not completed an initial USAR AGR tour or when voluntary separation is not in the best interests
of the USAR AGR program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text:
SECARMY determines that existing needs of the Service require delay of favorable separation action. The
retention period will be specified when the decision is made to delay the separation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text: President or Congress has declared a period of national emergency or war is imminent or in progress.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-13. Eligibility for voluntary separation
chunks.text:
Voluntary requests for separation under this regulation may be accepted only from an officer who is mentally
competent when the request is submitted. When an officer is medically incapacitated from further military service due
to a physical or mental condition, the officer’s case will be delayed until he or she recovers or the officer is processed
through medical channels.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text:
When a commissioned officer or warrant officer (except for chaplains, judge advocates, and Army Medical
Department (AMEDD) personnel) with less than 10 years active federal commissioned service (AFCS) submits a
request for a voluntary REFRAD under chapter 2 or an unqualified resignation under chapter 3 of this regulation, the
first colonel in the officer’s chain of command or supervision will counsel the officer. Judge advocates, chaplains, and
AMEDD officers will be counseled by a senior officer of their branch in the chain of technical supervision or as
specifically designated by their branch. If the officer is an RC officer on the active duty list (ADL) or an AGR officer,
and the officer is one-time nonselect for promotion, then the requirement for counseling is waived.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text: Counseling will include:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text: Advice concerning the opportunities available in a military career.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text: A discussion of the officer’s previously achieved investment in the Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text: A determination as to whether the officer has satisfied all applicable service obligations, as computed by HRC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text:
A determination that the officer is not under investigation or charges, awaiting results of trial, or being consid-
ered for administrative elimination.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text:
A determination that the officer is not AWOL, in the confinement of civil authorities, suffering from a severe
mental disease or defect, or in default in respect to public property or public funds.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text:
Advice encouraging a Regular Army (RA) officer to accept an appointment in the USAR. An RC officer will
be encouraged to retain commissioned status in the USAR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-14. Counseling
chunks.text:
Information concerning USAR career opportunities may be obtained from agencies listed in table 1 - 1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-15. Separation pay
chunks.text:
Eligibility for and payment of separation pay is governed by DODI 1332.29; DOD 7000.14 - R, Volume 7A; and AR
637 - 2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-16. Leave
chunks.text:
Commanders may grant leave in accordance with AR 600 - 8 - 10 in conjunction with an officer’s separation. However,
this leave must be taken prior to the effective date of separation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-17. Recoupment
chunks.text:
Individuals who participate in certain advanced education programs and fail to complete their incurred ADSO(s) (RA
and/or RC) are subject to the recoupment provisions of AR 37 - 104 - 4. Recoupment action by the servicing Finance
Office/Defense Military Pay Office must be accomplished prior to separation of an individual who has not fulfilled
the ADSO incurred as a result of participation in that advanced education program in accordance with applicable law
and regulations.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-18. Officer under investigation or pending court-martial
chunks.text:
An officer pending court-martial charges or investigation with a review toward court-martial will not be separated
without Headquarters, Department of the Army (HQDA) approval.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-19. Officer awaiting appellate review of adjudged dismissal or dishonorable discharge
chunks.text:
An officer who has been convicted and sentenced to dismissal or dishonorable discharge will not be discharged prior
to completion of appellate review without prior approval of CG, HRC. An RA officer may be processed for excess
leave under AR 600 - 8 - 10. An RC officer may be released from AD in accordance with paragraph 2 - 18.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text: personnel with access to sensitive programs

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text: The procedures in paragraph 1-20b apply to officers in the following categories:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text: Knowledge of sensitive compartmented information.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text: Nuclear Weapon Personnel Reliability Program assignment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text: Knowledge of Single Integrated Operational Plan-Extremely Sensitive Information.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text: Special Access Program knowledge.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text: Presidential Support assignment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-20. Action required prior to involuntary separation or under other than honorable discharge of
chunks.text:
Prior to involuntary separation or other than honorable discharge of personnel under paragraphs 1-20a(1)
through 1-20a(5), the individual’s commander/supervisor will coordinate with the supporting security official (Assis-
tant Chief of Staff (Intelligence), Intelligence Officer or U.S. Army Security Manager). Special security policies apply
that may impact the separation procedure. Separation/discharge will not be effected unless the security official concurs
with the action.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-21. Reassignment of an officer pending involuntary separation
chunks.text:
Unless otherwise directed by CG, HRC, an officer pending an involuntary separation action will not undergo a per-
manent change of station (PCS) reassignment until the case is closed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-22. Termination of appointment (active duty list officer only)
chunks.text:
Acceptance of the resignation or the discharge of officers in an RA or Reserve status, unless otherwise specified in
this regulation, will result in appropriate action being taken to terminate any warrant or temporary commission they
may be holding simultaneously. However, when the best interests of the Government and the individual will be served,
a warrant officer may, at the discretion of the accepting authority, be permitted to resign from a particular warrant or
commissioned status.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
When an officer’s tour of AD is terminated due to discharge, REFRAD, or resignation, the period of service will be
characterized as honorable, general (under honorable conditions), under other than honorable, dishonorable (warrant
officers who do not hold a commission only), or dismissal depending on the circumstances. The service of an officer
who retires may only be characterized as honorable. The characterization of service will be predicated on the officer’s
behavior and performance while a member of the Army. Characterization normally will be based on a pattern of
behavior and duty performance rather than an isolated incident. However, there are circumstances in which conduct
reflected by a single incident may provide the basis of characterization of service.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Honorable characterization of service. An officer will normally receive an honorable characterization of ser-
vice when the quality of the officer’s service has met the standards of acceptable conduct and performance of duty, or
the final revocation of a security clearance under DODI 5200.02 and AR 380 - 67 for reasons that do not involve acts
of misconduct for an officer. DD Form 256 (Honorable Discharge Certificate) will be furnished to a discharged officer;
however, a certificate is not issued when an officer is released from AD. When the separation is based solely on pre-
service activities, substandard performance of duty, or final revocation of a security clearance under DODI 5200.02
and AR 380 - 67 for reasons that do not involve acts of misconduct, it will be honorable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
General (under honorable conditions) characterization of service. An officer will normally receive an under
honorable conditions characterization of service when the officer’s military record is satisfactory but not sufficiently
meritorious to warrant an honorable discharge. A separation under honorable conditions will normally be appropriate
when an officer-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Submits an unqualified resignation or a request for REFRAD under circumstances involving misconduct.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Is separated based on misconduct, including misconduct for which punishment was imposed, which renders
the officer unsuitable for further service, unless an under other than honorable conditions separation is appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Is discharged for physical disability resulting from intentional misconduct or willful neglect, or which was
incurred during a period of unauthorized absence.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Is discharged for the final revocation of a security clearance under DODI 5200.02 and AR 380 - 67 as a result
of an act or acts of misconduct, including misconduct for which punishment was imposed, unless a discharge under
other than honorable conditions is appropriate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Under other than honorable conditions characterization of service. A discharge under other than honorable
conditions is an administrative separation from the service. A discharge certificate will not be issued. An officer will
normally receive an under other than honorable conditions when he or she-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Resigns for the good of the Service (see chap 3).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Is dropped from the rolls (DFR) of the Army in accordance with paragraph 5 - 9.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Is involuntarily separated due to misconduct, moral or professional dereliction, or for the final revocation of a
security clearance under DODI 5200.02 and AR 380 - 67 as a result of an act or acts of misconduct, including miscon-
duct for which punishment was imposed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Is discharged following conviction by civilian authorities (see para 2 - 14).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Dishonorable characterization of service. A general court-martial (GCM) may sentence a warrant officer who
is not commissioned to a dishonorable discharge. A discharge certificate will not be issued.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Undetermined characterization of service. An officer’s service will be characterized as “to be determined” when
released from AD when being processed for elimination action (see chap 4).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Dismissal. A GCM may sentence a commissioned officer to a dismissal.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: No formal discharge certificate. No formal discharge certificate will be issued when the officer is-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: DFR of the Army (see para 5 - 9).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Dismissed as a result of sentence of court-martial.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Removed under the criminal code of the United States.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Discharged under other than honorable conditions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Separated with a dishonorable discharge (applies only to a warrant officer who does not hold a commission).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text: Separated with a dismissal (applies only to commissioned officers).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-23. Types of administrative and judicial discharge/characterization of service
chunks.text:
Release or discharge from active duty. A DD Form 214 (Certificate of Release or Discharge from Active Duty)
will be furnished as prescribed in AR 635 - 8 to an officer who is separated from AD after completing 90 calendar days
of continuous AD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
The officer’s immediate commander will ensure that medical examination procedures are followed as stated in
AR 40 - 501.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
All RA, AGR, and RC officers on AD for 180 days or more are required to complete a separation history and
physical examination (SHPE) prior to voluntary or involuntary separation from AD or retirement pursuant to AR
40 - 501 and DODI 6040.46. The SHPE may be waived only in cases where the officer is not under military control of
the Army, such as unauthorized absence or civilian incarceration. In such cases, the officer’s immediate commander
will initiate a request for waiver of the SHPE and forward it through command channels to the CG, HRC (or Chief,
NGB for ARNG AGR officers) for endorsement to the SECARMY or designee. The request must provide justification
for waiver of the SHPE and include documentation that substantiates the officer is not under military control of the
Army.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
10 USC 1177 establishes that a medical examination is required before administrative separation for any officer
who has deployed overseas in support of a contingency operation or has been sexually assaulted during the previous
24 months, and is diagnosed as experiencing post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) or traumatic brain injury (TBI), or
reasonably asserts the influence of such a condition, based upon service while deployed. The purpose of the medical
evaluation is to evaluate a diagnosis of PTSD or TBI. Any such Soldier will not be administratively separated under
conditions other than honorable until the results of the medical examination have been reviewed by the separation
authority. The commander also must ensure that all specific requirements for a medical examination under 10 USC
1177 are followed under the provisions of AR 40 - 501.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
An officer, who has been deployed overseas in support of a contingency operation during the previous 24
months, and who is diagnosed by a physician, clinical psychologist, psychiatrist, licensed clinical social worker, or
psychiatric advanced practice registered nurse as experiencing PTSD or TBI or who otherwise reasonably alleges the
influence of such a condition based on the service of the officer while deployed or sexual assault, will not be admin-
istratively separated under conditions other than honorable, including an administrative separation in lieu of court-
martial, until the appropriate separation authority has reviewed and considered the results of the required medical
examination under 10 USC 1177 (see para 4-3d).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
An officer may only be retained past separation date for medical reasons when continued hospitalization or
medical observation is required to determine entitlement to retirement or separation for physical disability. An officer
being retired (voluntary or involuntary) due to maximum age (see para 6 - 22) or length of service (see para 6 - 23) will
not be retained on AD unless the medical condition requires referral to a physical evaluation board (PEB) (see para
6 - 7). An officer who is medically fit for retention will not be retained beyond the established separation date. An
officer will not be retained without written consent (see para 1-24i).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
The commander of the medical treatment facility (MTF) will forward requests for retention endorsed by the
officer’s unit commander through Headquarters, U.S. Army Medical Command (MCHO - CL), 2748 Worth Road,
Joint Base San Antonio-Fort Sam Houston, San Antonio, TX 78234 - 6000 to the Commander, Human Resources
Command (AHRC - OPD - A), 1600 Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400; or for AGR personnel
to the Commander, Human Resources Command (AHRC - AR), 1600 Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY
40122 - 5400, or for 10 USC AGR ARNGUS Soldiers to Chief, National Guard Bureau (ARNG - HCM), 111 S. George
Mason Drive, Arlington, VA 22204 - 1373, for approval. The requests will include the following information:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Officer’s name, rank, and Department of Defense identification (DODID) number.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Reason for release (for example, nonselection for promotion).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Scheduled release date.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Medical reasons for retention.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Medical facility point of contact (POC).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: A copy of completed DA Form 2823 (Sworn Statement) (see fig 1 - 1).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: The officer will be advised of the following retention advantages:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Medical care and/or hospitalization provided.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Normal benefits such as pay and allowances, to include continued post exchange and commissary privileges.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Dependent medical care when an officer is ordered to AD for more than 30 days.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
The additional active service is creditable for longevity and retirement. For Reserve officers ordered to AD for
30 days or less, any retention under paragraph 1-24e past the termination date of the orders is not creditable as AD
for retirement under 10 USC 12731 (see para 1-24h).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: State income tax benefits where allowed by law.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: Advise the officer that if he or she is eligible for disability separation, the advantages are as follows:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
If permanently retired, election of the most favorable retired pay allowed. If temporarily retired, the same se-
lection of pay is authorized, but the minimum will not be less than 50 percent of the basic pay.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
If retired, the officer, along with dependents, will be eligible for certain medical care when facilities and staffing
permit at uniformed services facilities and certain medical care in civilian facilities (see AR 40 - 3).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: The percentage of retired pay that is based on the disability may be exempt from taxation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text: If discharged for disability, the officer may be entitled to separation pay.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
The retained officer will sign the DA Form 2823 (see fig 1 - 1). The next of kin or legal representative will sign
for the retained officer when the retained officer is mentally incompetent or unable to sign. If the officer is unable to
sign and the next of kin or legal representative cannot be located or will not indicate whether the officer will be
retained, the officer will be retained until a retention decision is reached. The hospital commander will forward the
details of the case as required by paragraph 1-24f. File a copy in the officer’s Army military human resource record
(AMHRR).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-24. Medical examination/retention
chunks.text:
See AR 135 - 381 concerning procedures for RC officers on active duty for training (ADT) and active duty for
operational support (ADOS) tours of 30 calendar days or less and are hospitalized.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text:
A commissioned or warrant officer under investigation for an offense chargeable under the Uniform Code of
Military Justice (UCMJ) that could result in dismissal or punitive discharge may not be referred for or continue disa-
bility processing unless:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text: The investigation ends without charges.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text: The commander exercising proper court-martial jurisdiction dismisses the charges.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text:
The commander exercising proper court-martial jurisdiction refers the charge for trial to a court-martial that
cannot adjudge such a sentence.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text:
When a commissioned or warrant officer, as applicable, is being processed for one of the actions listed in para-
graphs 1-25b(1) through 1-25b(4), the officer will be processed in accordance with the provisions of this regulation
and through the Medical Evaluation Board (MEB)/PEB system pursuant to AR 635 - 40. If the result of the physical
disability evaluation is a finding of physical fitness, the Army Physical Disability Agency will approve the findings
for SECARMY and forward the proceedings to Commander, Human Resources Command (AHRC - OPD - A), 1600
Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400, to be processed with the other action. If a physical disability
evaluation results in a finding of physical unfitness, both actions will be forwarded by HRC (AHRC - OPD - A), to
SECARMY or designee for determination of appropriate disposition.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text: Referral to the DAADB except when the DAADB is convened as a result of an imposed reduction in force.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text: Involuntary REFRAD due to civil conviction or moral turpitude.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text: Referral for elimination under chapter 4 of this regulation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text:
Request for separation, resignation, or retirement in lieu of elimination.
Note. Generally, officers approved to resign for the good of the Service in lieu of trial by court-martial are ineligible
for referral to the MEB and PEB. However, if the officer was referred to the MEB prior to approval of the resignation,
the MEB and/or PEB must be completed and then action may be taken on the resignation request; Officers pending
administrative elimination are normally dual processed for the elimination action and completion of the Disability
Evaluation System. For dual processing to occur, referral to the MEB must occur before the date the DASA (RB)
approves the officer’s elimination pursuant to AR 635 - 40.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text:
When a physical disability evaluation of an officer processed for separation or retirement for the reasons indi-
cated in paragraphs 1-25b(1) through 1-25b(4) results in a finding of unfitness and a recommendation that the officer
be placed on the temporary disability retirement list (TDRL), SECARMY or designee may direct that the officer be
placed on the TDRL with the provision that if the officer is later removed in due course from the TDRL for disability
separation or retirement, the officer is to be retired, but if the officer is later removed in due course from the TDRL
because he is found fit, the officer is to be separated from the service, effective on the date of the officer’s removal
from the TDRL with a specified characterization of discharge. As in the case of an officer processed for separation or
retirement for the reasons indicated in paragraphs 1-25b(1) through 1-25b(4) whose physical disability evaluation
results in a finding of unfitness and a recommendation that the officer be separated or permanently retired, SECARMY
or designee may direct that either the separation or retirement action under this regulation or the disability action take
precedence.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text:
When an officer is processed for separation or retirement for reasons other than those indicated in paragraphs
1-25b(1) through 1-25b(4), physical disability takes precedence if the officer is retained with consent. The procedures
in paragraph 1 - 24 will be followed.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-25. Referral for physical disability evaluation
chunks.text:
Officers previously determined physically unfit and continued on AD under AR 635 - 40 must be referred for
disability evaluations at final retirement or separation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-26. Indebtedness
chunks.text:
An officer will not be retained on AD to satisfy an indebtedness to the Government or for collection of forfeitures
adjudged by court-martial. The officer’s final separation order will indicate the total amount of unpaid indebtedness
and forfeitures due the Government on the REFRAD date (see DOD 7000.14 - R, Volume 7A).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-27. Return from overseas
chunks.text:
An officer stationed outside continental United States (OCONUS), who has a pending separation action will not be
returned from the OCONUS command without HRC approval or, in the case of officers in special branches, without
notifying special branch personnel managers also.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-28. Assignment of personnel to a U.S. Army transition center
chunks.text: An officer stationed in CONUS who must be reassigned to a TC will be processed according to AR 635 - 8.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-28. Assignment of personnel to a U.S. Army transition center
chunks.text:
The OCONUS commander will issue orders returning the officer to a CONUS (or other area of residence) U.S.
Army TC according to AR 635 - 8, unless otherwise directed by CG, HRC. Consistent with availability of transporta-
tion, an officer will be returned only early enough to permit transfer processing for separation and normal travel time.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-28. Assignment of personnel to a U.S. Army transition center
chunks.text:
HRC (AHRC - OPD - A) will issue separation instructions on all other officers not later than 30 calendar days
before the scheduled release date.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-29. Separation requests/actions for Reserve Component
chunks.text:
Separation requests or actions not specifically covered in this regulation that pertain to an RC officer not on the ADL
will be routed to CG, HRC; for an ARNG (ARNGUS) officer, requests or actions will be routed to the appropriate
state adjutant general for processing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: Officers eligible for separation and serving in a foreign country may be separated in that country, provided the-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: Officer requests separation in that country.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: Officer’s separation in that country is not precluded by any other provision.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: Foreign government concerned has either formally or informally-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: Consented to the officer’s separation in its territory.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text:
Consented generally to the separation of an officer otherwise eligible for separation under paragraphs 1-30a(1)
and 1-30a(2).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text:
An officer will obtain all necessary documents for lawful presence in the foreign country prior to separation in
that country.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: The ACOM/ASCC/DRU commander may disapprove requests for separation in an OCONUS command when-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: Revocation action pursuant to AR 380 - 67 has been taken against the officer during current term of AD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: The officer’s access to defense information is suspended under AR 380 - 67.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text:
There is cogent reason that the officer’s presence in the OCONUS area in a nonmilitary status would endanger
U.S. national security.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-30. Separating an officer in a foreign country
chunks.text: Other cogent reasons exist that the officer should not be separated in the OCONUS command.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text:
An officer confined in a foreign penal institution pursuant to the sentence of a foreign court may be separated
from the Service during period of confinement only-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: With the approval of CG, HRC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: After final action (including final appellate action, if any) by the foreign authority.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: With the specific consent of the country concerned to the officer’s separation in its territory.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text:
When the OCONUS commander considers separating an officer before completing sentence to confinement, the
commander will forward the request for approval to Commander, Human Resources Command (AHRC - OPD - A),
1600 Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400, with the following documentation about the officer:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Name, grade, DODID, and branch of service.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Last organization and assignment.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Offense(s) alleged to have been committed and the related facts and circumstances.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Court that tried the officer.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Date and place of trial.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Offense(s) of which the officer was convicted.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: The officer’s sentence.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Matters in mitigation, extenuation, or aggravation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Appellate action, if any, and result.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Whether the action of the foreign court is final or whether further appellate action is possible or contemplated.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Place and condition of confinement.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Possibility for parole and pertinent facts.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text: Special facts and circumstances, including reasons supporting discharge during confinement.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text:
Whether consent for separation in the territory of the foreign country concerned has been obtained from that
country.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-31. Officer confined pursuant to the sentence in a foreign court
chunks.text:
While an officer is confined in a penal institution, commanders may initiate action for the officer to be discharged
upon release from confinement and returned to the United States or its territorial possessions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-32. Separation while under investigation or sentenced but not confined by a foreign court
chunks.text:
Only after final action by the foreign country can the following officers be considered for separation under
paragraphs 1 - 30 or 1 - 31, whichever is appropriate:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-32. Separation while under investigation or sentenced but not confined by a foreign court
chunks.text:
An officer whose sentence to confinement by a foreign court is not suspended and who is not confined pending
appellate action.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-32. Separation while under investigation or sentenced but not confined by a foreign court
chunks.text:
An officer who is not confined but is charged with or is under investigation for an offense that is subject to the
jurisdiction of foreign authorities that could lead to confinement.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-32. Separation while under investigation or sentenced but not confined by a foreign court
chunks.text:
An officer who was accepted for separation in a foreign country but who is not stationed in that country may be
returned to the place of acceptance and be separated from there if in compliance with paragraph 1 - 30.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-33. Discharge based on conscientious objection
chunks.text: Process according to AR 600 - 43.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-34. Inter-Service transfer of Army commissioned officers on the active duty list
chunks.text: Process according to AR 614 - 120.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text:
When recommending an administrative separation for any officer for any reason, including retirement, covered
by this regulation, commanders must ensure the separation packet contains a statement signed by the officer, with the
officer’s answers to the following questions (see AR 600 - 20):

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text: Did you file an unrestricted report of a sexual assault in which you were a victim within the past 24 months?

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text:
If the answer to paragraph 1-35a(1) is YES, do you believe that this separation action is a direct or indirect
result of your sexual assault, or your reporting of the sexual assault?

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text:
The officer’s General Court-Martial Convening Authority (GCMCA) or higher authority will review all admin-
istrative separations involving known victims of sexual assault and officers who answered YES to any of the questions
cited on their signed statement as stated in paragraph 1-35a. The reviewing authority will determine-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text:
Does the separation appear to be in retaliation resulting from the officer filing an unrestricted sexual assault
report? If so, consult with your local staff judge advocate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text:
Does separation involve a medical condition that is related to the sexual assault? If so, consult with the appro-
priate medical command personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text:
Is the separation in the best interest of the Army, the Servicemember, or both? If not, consult with your local
staff judge advocate.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 1 > 1-35. Screening and counseling of victims of sexual assault
chunks.text:
Commanders will include a statement that the separation was reviewed in accordance with paragraphs 1-35a
and 1-35b.
Figure 1 - 1. Sample format for retention statement used on DA Form 2823
Figure 1 - 2. Sample memorandum for victim of sexual assault statement for administrative separation

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2
chunks.text: Release from Active Duty of Reserve Component Commissioned and Warrant Officers

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-1. Scope
chunks.text:
This chapter sets forth the rules for REFRAD of RC officers and officers of the Army without specification of com-
ponent, including those assigned to other than DA agencies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text:
Commanders listed in paragraphs 2-2a through 2-2c are known as separation approval authorities (SAAs). The SAA
cannot disapprove a request (with the exception of HRC for USAR AGR officers) but will make a recommendation
and forward to Commander, Human Resources Command (AHRC - OPD - A), 1600 Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort
Knox, KY 40122 - 5400; The Judge Advocate General (DAJA - PT), 2200 Army Pentagon, Washington, DC
20310 - 2200; or Office of the Chief of Chaplains (DACH - PER), 2700 Army Pentagon, Washington, DC 20310 - 2700,
as applicable; or for USAR AGR officers, to Commander, Human Resources Command (AHRC - AR), 1600 Spear-
head Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400. The CG, HRC or equivalent authority is the SAA for all fully
qualified requests outside the provisions of Stop Loss; however, cases involving ADSO waivers may either be ap-
proved/disapproved by the appropriate waiver level or the requested separation date may be deferred to the end of the
respective ADSO to prevent the officer from reapplying for REFRAD. This authority will not be further delegated
without prior approval of SECARMY.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: General officers in command of Army medical centers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: Commanders of units and installations having GCM authority.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: Commanders of:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: Personnel centers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: Training centers.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: OCONUS replacement depots.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: All Army installations authorized 4,000 or more AD military personnel.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: HRC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: Unless otherwise stated, the appropriate related paragraph will be cited as the separation authority.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-2. Separation approval authority
chunks.text: The related separation program designator (SPD) codes are provided in AR 635 - 5 - 1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Status upon separation
chunks.text: Unless discharged, officers holding a Reserve appointment will retain their current permanent grade in the RC.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Status upon separation
chunks.text:
Service academy graduates and Reserve Officers’ Training Corps (ROTC) distinguished military graduates
(DMGs) who are released/separated from AD before completing their ADSO will complete the remainder of their
ADSO in the Selected Reserve. This requirement may only be waived by ASA (M&RA) or their delegate if it is
determined that no unit vacancy is available for the officer. The Selected Reserve includes troop units of the ARNGUS
and Army Reserve, the AGR Program, and Individual Mobilization Augmentation Program.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Status upon separation
chunks.text:
AR 135 - 91 defines various statutory and contractual service obligations (to include methods of fulfillment)
incurred by Reserve personnel on initial entry into military service. Officers not continued on AD in another status
will be released from AD and returned to the ARNGUS or USAR by separation orders.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-3. Status upon separation
chunks.text:
An officer must serve in the Armed Forces until completion of the statutory military service obligation (MSO)
(see 10 USC 651). In accordance with DODI 1304.25, an officer has an 8-year MSO. REFRAD under the provisions
of chapter 2 of this regulation does not terminate the MSO. An officer who requests REFRAD before completing the
8-year MSO must complete the MSO in an RC, or submit a resignation under chapter 3 of this regulation with a request
for waiver of the MSO by the ASA (M&RA). To waive an officer’s MSO, the ASA (M&RA) must find that the officer
has no potential for service under conditions of full mobilization.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-4. Years of service
chunks.text:
For the purpose of this chapter, years of service applies to all periods of service in which the officer held an
appointment as a commissioned officer, other than constructive service and service as a warrant officer.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-4. Years of service
chunks.text:
Travel time will be included in years of service computation for officers who are ordered to AD exceeding 30
calendar days (see 37 USC 204).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
If eligible under the criteria prescribed in paragraph 2-5b, an officer may request REFRAD whenever such
action is considered appropriate (see fig 2 - 1). Unless otherwise specified, application for REFRAD will be submitted
not earlier than 12 months or no later than 6 months before the desired release date or beginning date of transition
leave, whichever is the earliest. The officer’s immediate (or higher) commander/supervisor may waive the 6-month
requirement if the needs of the Army are met through a change in mission requirement or the assignment of a replace-
ment officer.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text: To be eligible for REFRAD under this paragraph, an officer must have-

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
Completed the current period of service required by law or the minimum period required by the program that
placed the officer on AD as a commissioned or warrant officer.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text: Fulfilled the following additional service requirements, as applicable:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
Officers who have departed the CONUS normally will be required to complete the current prescribed tour for
the areas of assignment as specified in AR 614 - 30, unless returned earlier under existing policies.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
Officers who have attended service schools or civilian educational institutions or participated in special training
for which a utilization tour is required, or who have received continuation or accession pay, normally will complete
the period of service specified. The period of service will be as specified by AR 350 - 100 and other appropriate regu-
lations under which officers applied for or received such schooling or benefits.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
Officers on orders for assignment to a military mission, a Military Assistance Advisory Group, or attaché office
must complete their training and the OCONUS tour of duty prescribed by AR 614 - 30.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text: All other officers who receive a PCS will be required to serve 1 year at their new duty station.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
Service academy graduates and ROTC DMG who are released/separated from AD before completing their
ADSO will complete the remainder of their ADSO in the selected Reserve. This requirement may only be waived by
the ASA (M&RA) or designee.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
In all other cases, HQDA reserves the right to require compliance with orders to a course of instruction that will
result in an ADSO or reassignment orders in CONUS or to any OCONUS assignment, if such orders are announced
prior to submission of application for REFRAD. However, exception requests will be considered on an individual
basis provided the application for REFRAD is received by commanders exercising GCMCA jurisdiction or command-
ers at a comparable level of command. The exception request must be forwarded within 30 calendar days of receipt
of the alert or reassignment order, whichever is earlier, and the requested effective date cannot be more than 6 months
subsequent to date of application. Officers who are service obligated beyond 6 months may submit their application
for REFRAD, as an exception to policy, more than 6 months in advance of the requested effective date. The nonstat-
utory criteria in this paragraph may be waived only by HQDA on a case-by-case basis.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
If the application is approved, HRC (AHRC - OPD - A) (or HRC (AHRC - AR) for USAR AGR officers) will
forward separation instructions to the appropriate MPD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
If disapproved, HRC (AHRC - OPD - A), TJAG (DAJA - PT), or Office of the Chief of Chaplains (DACH - PER),
as applicable, will return the application to the officer through channels.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
The steps required for processing voluntary REFRAD due to personal reasons are shown in table 2 - 1.

Recommendation for approval or disapproval and character of service. Include complete justification
when approval is recommended and the officer has not fulfilled an ADSO. Also include justification for dis-
approval.

chunks.text:
Statement that the officer is not under investigation or charges, awaiting result of trial, being considered
for administrative elimination, AWOL, or under the control of civil authorities.

chunks.text:
Date officer departed CONUS or other area of residence for OCONUS assignment. Date of arrival of
dependents, whether at government or personal expense, whether logistical support was furnished, and
the rotation date, if applicable.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
Reviews and makes recommendation. Request will be forwarded by commanders exercising GCMCA
jurisdiction and commanders at a comparable level of command to Human Resources Command
(AHRC - OPD - A), 1600 Spearhead Division Avenue, Fort Knox, KY 40122 - 5400; The Judge Advocate
General (DAJA - PT), 2200 Army Pentagon, Washington, DC 20310 - 2200; or Office of the Chief of Chap-
lains (DACH - PER), 2700 Army Pentagon, Washington, DC 20310 - 2700, as applicable, for final action.
Approved actions will be forwarded to the HRC (AHRC - OPD - A), for separation message processing.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-5. Voluntary release from active duty due to personal reasons
chunks.text:
For USAR AGR officers, the commander exercising GCM authority and commanders at a comparable
level of command will send requests to the HRC (AHRC - AR) for final action/separation processing.
SACT (TC)
Receives separation instructions and separates the officer. Final release orders and separation forms will
cite regulatory authority and SPD as shown in AR 635 - 5 - 1.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Voluntary release from active duty due to expiration of active duty commitment
chunks.text:
Applications for REFRAD will be submitted not earlier than 12 months or less than 6 months before the desired
release date or beginning date of transition leave, whichever is the earliest. The officer’s immediate (or higher) com-
mander/supervisor may waive the 6-month requirement if the needs of the Army are met through a change in mission
requirement or the assignment of a replacement officer.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Voluntary release from active duty due to expiration of active duty commitment
chunks.text:
The SAA will forward separation instructions to the appropriate MPD and ensure that an officer under jurisdic-
tion is released as scheduled.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Voluntary release from active duty due to expiration of active duty commitment
chunks.text:
An officer not under the jurisdiction of a commander having SAA will be reported to HRC (AHRC - OPD - A)
not later than 60 calendar days prior to scheduled date of REFRAD. USAR AGR officers will be reported to HRC
(AHRC - AR) or HRC (AHRC - OPD - A) or HRC (AHRC - AR) for USAR AGR officers. HRC will forward separation
instructions to the appropriate MPD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-6. Voluntary release from active duty due to expiration of active duty commitment
chunks.text:
The steps required for processing an officer’s voluntary REFRAD on expiration of AD commitment are shown
in table 2 - 2.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Voluntary release from active duty due to hardship
chunks.text:
An officer may be released from AD due to extreme personal or Family hardship. Hardship exists in circum-
stances involving death or disability of a member of the Soldier’s (or spouse’s) immediate Family, or when separation
from the Service will materially affect the care or support of the Family by alleviating undue and genuine hardship.
The officer must clearly establish that one of the following applies:

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Voluntary release from active duty due to hardship
chunks.text: The hardship is permanent and did not exist prior to entry on AD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Voluntary release from active duty due to hardship
chunks.text:
If the hardship existed prior to entry on AD, the condition has since intensified and can only be alleviated by
separating from AD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Voluntary release from active duty due to hardship
chunks.text: Upon REFRAD the officer will be able to eliminate or materially alleviate the condition.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Voluntary release from active duty due to hardship
chunks.text:
If the application has been disapproved by CG, HRC; TJAG; or Office of the Chief of Chaplains (OCCH), a
new application will not be submitted under the same authority unless there is new material evidence to support reap-
plication.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Voluntary release from active duty due to hardship
chunks.text:
If the application is approved, HRC (AHRC - OPD - A) or HRC (AHRC - AR), as applicable, will forward sepa-
ration instructions to the appropriate MPD. If the officer was commissioned through a funded program (for example,
AMEDD, Funded Legal Education Program (FLEP), U.S. Military Academy (USMA), ROTC, or a special procure-
ment program) or is a training recipient, recoupment may be required on a prorated basis.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-7. Voluntary release from active duty due to hardship
chunks.text:
If the application is disapproved, HRC (AHRC - OPD - A), TJAG (DAJA - PT), OCCH (DACH - PER), or HRC
(AHRC - AR), as applicable, will return the application to the officer through channels.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Voluntary release from active duty essential to national interest
chunks.text:
An officer may request REFRAD to provide more valuable service (in civilian capacity) to the nation. The
request must be motivated by national interest and not personal desire (see fig 2 - 1).

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Voluntary release from active duty essential to national interest
chunks.text: An officer elected or appointed to Congress will be released without application.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Voluntary release from active duty essential to national interest
chunks.text:
An officer appointed to a federal court or federal or state public office or elected to a public office of a state may
be released from AD on written request, provided the evidence establishes that the office to which appointed or elected
requires full-time service. Officers commissioned through a funded program (for example, AMEDD, FLEP, USMA,
ROTC, or special procurement programs), will not be released until completion of that initial Service school obliga-
tion.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Voluntary release from active duty essential to national interest
chunks.text:
If the application is approved, HRC (AHRC - OPD - A) (or HRC (AHRC - AR) for USAR AGR officers) will
forward separation instructions to the appropriate MPD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-8. Voluntary release from active duty essential to national interest
chunks.text:
If disapproved, HRC (AHRC - OPD - A), TJAG (DAJA - PT), OCCH (DACH - PER); or HRC (AHRC - AR), as
applicable, will return the application to the officer through channels.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
A commander with SAA may release an RC officer who requests REFRAD because of pregnancy provided the
officer has no ADSO. This authority may not be further delegated. When the SAA approves the REFRAD, release
instructions will be forwarded to the appropriate MPD. Should the SAA disapprove the REFRAD request, the SAA
will make a recommendation to include justification and forward the request to the office listed in paragraphs 1-10b,
1-10d, or 1-10f, as applicable, for final action.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
For an officer not under the jurisdiction of a commander having SAA, the approval authority is HRC, TJAG, or
OCCH (or HRC (AHRC - AR) for USAR AGR officers). On approved applications, HRC (AHRC - OPD - A) (or HRC
(AHRC - AR) for USAR AGR officers) will forward REFRAD instructions to the appropriate MPD.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
When it has been determined that an officer is pregnant, the officer will be counseled by the officer’s immediate
commanding officer or executive officer. The counselor will explain to the officer that the purpose of the counseling
is to provide information concerning the officer’s rights, entitlements, and responsibilities with respect to continued
AD or separation. The counselor will not influence or direct the officer to make any particular decision. A statement
of counseling will be signed by the counselor (see fig 2 - 2). A copy of the statement of counseling will be filed in the
officer’s military personnel file (MPF) or AMHRR.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
An officer who is commissioned through a funded program or a training recipient (for example, AMEDD, FLEP,
USMA, ROTC, or special procurement programs) will not be released until completion of that initial Service school
obligation. An officer serving under a contractual agreement, such as but not limited to incentive special pay, medical
additional special pay, medical officer retention bonus, or nurse accession bonus, will not be released until completion
of the service obligation. In addition, an officer will not be released from AD until the compulsory obligation acquired
as a result of completing flight school is served. However, when extenuating circumstances exist, an officer may
request a hardship separation in accordance with paragraph 2 - 7.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
An officer who remains on AD will be assigned to an area where concurrent travel of dependents is authorized
until completion of the initial service obligation.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
If, before the REFRAD is accomplished, a medical officer determines that a pregnancy has terminated for any
reason (including birth) the authority for separation no longer exists.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text: A pregnant officer may request leave of absence per AR 600 - 8 - 10.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
An officer may request a specific separation date. However, the separation authority and the officer’s military
physician will determine the separation date. The date must not be later than 30 calendar days before the expected
date of delivery, or the date is the latest date the officer’s military physician will authorize the officer to travel to home
of record or entry on AD location, whichever date is earlier.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
An officer released under honorable conditions who is pregnant at the time of release is entitled to maternity care
per AR 40 - 400. These medical benefits are limited to care in military hospitals and do not, regardless of circum-
stances, include care obtained in civilian hospitals.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
Characterization of service for REFRAD due to pregnancy may be characterized as honorable or under honorable
conditions.

chunks.heading_path: Chapter 2 > 2-9. Voluntary release from active duty due to pregnancy
chunks.text:
The steps required for processing voluntary REFRAD due to pregnancy are shown in table 2 - 5.
